english: Sticky Rice Ball with Sweet Filling

food_subgroup: Glutinous rice confection / sweet rice ball

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Ai Wo Wo exists; closest_parent is the glutinous rice food product class (FOODON_03315615).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Ai Wo Wo; A0CKP (rice-based confectionery) is the nearest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Ai Wo Wo or its immediate category.

allergens: gluten (glutinous rice contains trace gluten risk; cross-contamination possible)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Ai Wo Wo is 6 pieces, weighing approximately 180 g, as sold by restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Glutinous Rice Ball with Sweet Bean Filling — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite; adapted for Ai Wo Wo profile
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Ai Wo Wo exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked glutinous rice (FDC ID 168872) and red bean paste (FDC ID 174289), weighted approximately 60% glutinous rice shell and 40% sweet bean/sesame filling, consistent with traditional Ai Wo Wo composition. Energy and macros are cross-checked against published Chinese food composition data (Yang Yuexin et al., China Food Composition Tables, 6th ed., 2018) for similar glutinous rice confections.

gi_source.title: Glutinous rice — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to Ai Wo Wo
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ai Wo Wo. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice (GI ~65, medium category), which forms the dominant structural component (~60%) of Ai Wo Wo. The sweet bean paste filling contributes additional simple sugars, supporting a medium GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 82.8) / 100 ≈ 53.8, where carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 46.0 × 1.8 = 82.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~18 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Ai Wo Wo is very low in sodium (~32 mg per serving), making it acceptable for individuals managing hypertension, though portion size should be monitored due to its moderate caloric density.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content with medium glycemic index (GI ~65) and high glycemic load per serving (~53.8)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid Ai Wo Wo due to its high carbohydrate load (~82.8 g per 6-piece serving) and medium GI, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, portion should be restricted to 1–2 pieces with a low-GI meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and zero cholesterol; moderate total carbohydrate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ai Wo Wo is low in saturated fat (~0.5 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, posing minimal direct cardiovascular risk from lipid perspective, though its high sugar content warrants moderation in individuals with metabolic syndrome or dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ai Wo Wo (艾窝窝, ài wō wō) is a traditional Chinese glutinous rice confection originating from Beijing, historically favoured by the imperial court during the Ming Dynasty. It consists of a snow-white, spherical outer shell made from steamed glutinous rice that is pounded and shaped by hand, encasing a sweet filling typically composed of red bean paste, sesame paste, or crushed nuts mixed with sugar. The texture is soft, sticky, and yielding, with a delicate sweetness and mild fragrance. Each piece is small and round, and the dish is traditionally served in sets of six. Nutritionally, Ai Wo Wo is a carbohydrate-dense food (~46 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with moderate energy (~220 kcal per 100 g), low fat (~2.5 g), low sodium (~18 mg), and modest protein (~3.8 g). Its medium glycemic index (~65) and high glycemic load per standard serving make it a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume with caution. It is naturally dairy-free, egg-free, and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. In Singapore, it is served as a dessert or snack in Chinese restaurants and is associated with festive and cultural dining occasions.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Ai Wo Wo was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked glutinous rice and red bean paste, weighted by typical composition ratios, and cross-checked against Chinese food composition literature. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, the source ingredients are well-characterised, and the resulting values are internally consistent.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy (cooked glutinous rice FDC 168872; red bean paste FDC 174289) weighted ~60:40 shell-to-filling ratio, cross-checked against China Food Composition Tables 6th ed. (Yang Yuexin et al., 2018) for similar glutinous rice confections. No exact Ai Wo Wo entry exists in any approved primary database. 2. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice; no direct GI measurement for Ai Wo Wo was found in peer-reviewed literature. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based steamed confection with no hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-derived. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; glutinous rice and bean paste contain negligible vitamin C. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food not fortified with vitamin D. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for these ingredient categories. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source estimate. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source estimate. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source estimate. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source estimate. 5. The serving size of 180 g (6 pieces) is based on the field survey data listing description ('6 Pcs') and a typical per-piece weight of ~30 g for this style of confection. 6. Allergen note: sesame is a declared allergen. Glutinous rice is technically gluten-free by botanical classification, but cross-contamination risk in commercial kitchens means is_gluten_free is set to false as a precaution. 7. Multilingual names for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are transliterations of the Mandarin name as no distinct Malay, Tamil, or Hindi culinary names exist for this dish in Singapore; this is standard practice for imported Chinese confections.
